turns.content: I'm trying to write a function that takes an iterator and collects it into a Vec, but I keep thinking I need to manually call iter.collect() every time. Is there a way to make this generic so other types can do the same thing without rewriting logic?

That's a great intuition for API design, but you've hit on a common misconception: you don't actually need to manually call collect every single time if you implement the Extend trait. By implementing Extend<T> for your collection type, you can use the extend method directly or rely on iter.collect() which internally uses the trait implementation under the hood.
Oh I see, so I just add a method? But wait, doesn't that mean my collection now implements something like IntoIterator too since it has to work with iterators?

You've correctly identified a potential overlap, but they're not exactly the same thing. Your collection implementing Extend<T> allows it to consume an iterator and grow itself, while IntoIterator is about yielding items one by one when you call iter.next(). You can definitely have both traits on the same type without conflict.

I'm building a small engine and trying to avoid manual loops, so I keep thinking the way collections work in Python is exactly how it should be for Rust. My plan is just to have my custom data type implement Extend<T> so that iter.collect() works out of the box without me writing a for loop.

That's actually a common trap, but it's not quite how Rust idioms work. When you implement Extend<T>, you're defining what happens when something is added to a collection via the add method or the extend method on iterators, which allows iter.collect() to push items directly into your type without an explicit loop in user code.
But isn't that exactly what I want? If my struct implements Extend<T>, then every time I call extend on an iterator, it should just append those values to the vector automatically, right?

You're correct about the behavior once implemented, but there's a subtle distinction between what you expect and how the compiler enforces safety. The trait requires that your type be mutable or owned by the caller because extend modifies the collection in place during iteration.
So I can't just wrap my read-only struct in a vector? Does that mean I have to clone everything when I want to use iter.collect()? That feels like extra work compared to Python's list comprehension.

Exactly, and here is the analogy: think of Rust collections as immutable data structures until you explicitly allow mutation via traits. If your type doesn't implement Extend or isn't a mutable collection like Vec<T>, then iter.collect() will fail because it needs to mutate the target. You don't have to change everything, but if you want that Python-like flow without loops, your type must expose mutability through Implementing Extend.
